title: Graph Representation: Adjacency List and Matrix
description: Beyond trees -- nodes that can connect to ANY other node, not just two children, represented two different ways in code.
objectives: Understand a graph as nodes (vertices) and connections (edges), more general than a tree
Represent a graph with an adjacency list (List of Lists / Map of Lists)
Represent the same graph with an adjacency matrix, and compare trade-offs

sections.text: Every tree in the Trees module had a strict rule: each node has AT MOST two children (or, for a Trie, one parent), and there's exactly one path from the root to any node. A graph drops these restrictions entirely -- any node can connect to any number of other nodes, in any pattern, including cycles.
A collection of vertices (nodes) connected by edges, with no restriction on how many connections a vertex can have or what shape those connections form.

sections.title: Social network analogy
sections.text: A tree is like a strict company org chart -- one boss per person. A graph is like a friendship network -- anyone can be friends with anyone, friendships can form loops (A-B-C-A), and there's no single 'root' friend everyone descends from.
2. Adjacency List: A List of Neighbors per Node
A graph representation where each node stores a list of its directly connected neighbors -- commonly a List of Lists, or a Map<Node, List<Node>> for non-integer node identities.

adjList.get(0) is [1, 2] -- node 0 connects directly to nodes 1 and 2. For an UNDIRECTED edge (a connection with no direction, like a two-way friendship), we add the connection in BOTH directions, as addEdge does here.
3. Adjacency Matrix: A Grid of Connections
A graph representation using an n x n 2D array (recall 2D Arrays), where a 1 (or edge weight) at [i][j] means an edge exists between node i and node j, and 0 means no edge.
adjMatrix[0][1] = 1 and adjMatrix[1][0] = 1 both being set represents the same undirected edge -- notice the matrix here is symmetric across its diagonal, which is always true for undirected graphs.

sections.title: Adjacency list vs adjacency matrix
rows: O(V + E) -- proportional to actual edges
O(V^2) -- always, regardless of edge count
O(degree of u) -- scan u's neighbor list
List all of a node's neighbors
O(degree of u) -- already a list
O(V) -- must scan the entire row
SPARSE graphs (few edges relative to nodes)
DENSE graphs, or when edge-existence checks are frequent

sections.text: Most real-world graphs (social networks, road maps, dependency graphs) are SPARSE -- each node connects to relatively few others, not everyone. For these, adjacency list's O(V + E) space is far more efficient than a matrix's guaranteed O(V^2), which is why adjacency list is the more common default choice in practice, including for the BFS and DFS lessons coming up next.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to add the edge in BOTH directions for an undirected graph -- adjList.get(u).add(v) alone only represents a one-way (directed) connection.
Using an adjacency matrix by default for large, sparse graphs, wasting O(V^2) space when most of the matrix would just be zeros.
Confusing a graph's generality with a tree's restrictions -- a graph can have cycles and multiple paths between nodes; a tree cannot.
Assuming adjacency list gives O(1) edge-existence checks like a matrix does -- checking if v is a neighbor of u requires scanning u's list, not a direct lookup.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the key structural difference between a graph and a tree?
questions.answer: A tree restricts each node to at most a fixed number of children (e.g. 2 for binary trees) with exactly one path from the root to any node and no cycles. A graph has no such restriction -- any node can connect to any number of others, and cycles are allowed.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of an adjacency list vs an adjacency matrix?
questions.answer: An adjacency list uses O(V + E) space, proportional to the actual number of vertices and edges. An adjacency matrix always uses O(V^2) space, regardless of how many edges actually exist.

questions.question: When would an adjacency matrix be preferred over an adjacency list?
questions.answer: When the graph is dense (edges close to the maximum possible) or when frequent O(1) edge-existence checks ("is there an edge between u and v?") matter more than saving space -- for sparse graphs, an adjacency list is generally more efficient.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A graph generalizes a tree by removing restrictions on how nodes connect -- any node can link to any number of others, cycles are allowed, and there's no single root. Adjacency list represents this as a list of neighbors per node, using O(V + E) space -- efficient for the sparse graphs common in practice. Adjacency matrix represents it as an n x n grid where a 1 at [i][j] marks an edge, using O(V^2) space always, but giving O(1) edge-existence checks. Adjacency list is the more common default, especially for sparse graphs, and is what the upcoming BFS and DFS lessons build on.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Adjacency list = each person's own contact list -- only stores who THEY actually know. Adjacency matrix = a full seating chart marking every possible pair -- checks are instant, but the chart's size never shrinks even if most seats are empty.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: How does a graph differ structurally from a tree?
What is the space complexity of an adjacency list vs an adjacency matrix?
Why do you need to add an edge in both directions for an undirected graph's adjacency list?
When would you prefer an adjacency matrix over an adjacency list?
